column1: # OWASP ZAP baseline-scan rule suppressions for wl_manager.
# Format (tab-separated, ZAP standard):
#   <rule_id>
# Actions:
#   IGNORE  — suppress the finding entirely (treat as known-accepted)
#   WARN    — keep at WARN level (informational; doesn't fail the run)
#   FAIL    — escalate to FAIL (causes ZAP exit code != 0 and CI failure)
# Default if a rule isn't listed here: WARN.
# Workflow setting `-l FAIL` means only FAIL-level findings fail CI;
# WARN findings get reported in the HTML/Markdown artifacts.
# Maintenance rule: every IGNORE entry MUST cite (1) the rule's
# title, (2) the Splunk-specific reason it's acceptable, and (3)
# the date it was added. Don't suppress a finding just because it's
# noisy — explain WHY it's acceptable for this app.
# First-run triage outcome (2026-05-12)
# First scan ran locally against http://host.docker.internal:8000/en-US/account/login.
# Summary: FAIL-NEW=0, WARN-NEW=9 (unique rule IDs: 14 across all severities).
# Triage outcome: ALL findings are on Splunk's pre-auth surface
# (login page, error pages, robots.txt, sitemap.xml, bundled static
# assets). None touch our app code at /custom/wl_manager/. All 14
# are suppressed below with documented Splunk-specific rationale.
# IMPORTANT GAP — authenticated scanning:
# The baseline scan targets the login page and never authenticates,
# so it covers only Splunk's pre-auth surface and not our app code.
# To audit our handler + dashboards, ZAP needs an auth script
# (zap-baseline.py -U context.context auth). Adding that requires:
#   1. A ZAP context file (.zap/context.xml) describing the login form
#   2. A test user credential pair injected via env vars
#   3. Updating the workflow to pass -U <context> -n <context.xml>
# Out of scope for this triage round — flagged as future work.
# Suppressions

column3: Cookie No HttpOnly Flag — Splunk's session cookies (cval, splunkweb_csrf_token_8000). Splunk's bundled session manager controls these; setting HttpOnly is a Splunk web.conf change, not a wl_manager change. The CSRF token cookie is intentionally JS-readable so the frontend can include it as a header. (2026-05-12)

Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set — Splunk doesn't ship a CSP on its login page or default response. Adding a CSP is a Splunk web.conf customization (http_header_X-Content-Security-Policy) that affects the entire Splunk instance, not just our app. Out of scope for an app-level baseline. (2026-05-12)

Big Redirect Detected — Splunk's 303 redirects on /sitemap.xml, root, and search routes embed long return URLs with internal request IDs. Splunk-bundled redirect behavior; the "sensitive info" is internal Splunk request tracking, not application data. (2026-05-12)
Non-Storable Content — Splunk emits Cache-Control: no-store on login responses. Informational only; correct behavior for an auth page. (2026-05-12)

Cookie without SameSite Attribute — Splunk's session cookies don't set SameSite. Same root cause as 10010 — Splunk's bundled session manager. The CSRF cookie reaching same-origin handlers is intentional. (2026-05-12)

Permissions Policy Header Not Set — Splunk default response headers don't include Permissions-Policy. Could be added via web.conf if we want to lock down camera/microphone/geolocation, but those are already unused by Splunk Web. Low payoff. (2026-05-12)
Base64 Disclosure — Splunk's login page inlines bookmark/favicon images as base64 data URLs. Not sensitive content; standard SPA optimization. (2026-05-12)

Timestamp Disclosure - Unix — Splunk's login page emits its build timestamp in inline JS for cache-busting. Same value is in /en-US/static/@HASH/ static-asset URLs; trivial to discover by inspection. Not a real leak. (2026-05-12)
Modern Web Application — Pure informational; ZAP just notes that the target uses modern JS (SPA navigation). No action. (2026-05-12)
Dangerous JS Functions — Splunk's bundled JS uses eval/Function for templating. Owned by Splunk's vendor bundle. Replacing it is an upstream Splunk change. (2026-05-12)
Session Management Response Identified — ZAP notes session cookies appear in responses. Standard for any authenticated app. Informational. (2026-05-12)

Cross-Origin-Embedder/Opener/Resource Policy Missing — Splunk's default response headers don't set COEP/COOP/CORP. Configurable via web.conf for cross-origin isolation, but Splunk Web doesn't currently use cross-origin features that require these. Low payoff. (2026-05-12)

Sec-Fetch-* Headers Missing — These are REQUEST headers the browser should send, not response headers Splunk should set. ZAP flags servers that don't reject requests missing them, but most browsers always send them now. Informational. (2026-05-12)
Full Path Disclosure — Splunk's 404 error pages leak /opt/splunk/share/splunk/search_mrsparkle paths. This is Splunk's bundled error template; not configurable from app metadata. (2026-05-12)
